prompt: Develop a FieldTrip Ops Governance & Claims system for offline travel itinerary booking operations, content governance, and after‑sales resolution on Android. The app supports four roles: Traveler (creates itineraries and requests changes), Agent (books, reschedules, and itemizes fees), Reviewer (moderates content and evidence), and Administrator (configures SLAs, rules, and retention). Travelers create and edit itineraries with a step-by-step Android Views flow that works without connectivity, including traveler name initials, party size, dates (MM/DD/YYYY), and optional notes; before booking, the app validates local inventory and quotas (e.g., “2 seats remaining” or “sold out”) and prevents overbooking. Orders follow a strict state machine: Draft → Pending Confirmation → Booked → In Service → Completed, with Cancelled and Closed as terminal states; any Pending Confirmation order auto-closes after 30 minutes of inactivity, releasing quota, and any reschedule request must be made at least 24 hours before start time unless an Agent records an exception reason. Fee itemization is required for every booking and change, separating base fare, taxes/fees, and adjustments; refunds compute from stored rules (for example, cancel >48 hours: 100% refund; 24–48 hours: 50%; <24 hours: nonrefundable) and record approver name and timestamp. After-sales tickets support refund-only and return-and-refund style workflows (adapted to travel as “service not delivered” vs “partial delivery”), require at least one proof attachment (photo/PDF up to 10 MB), and run rule-based decisions such as eligibility timeouts (claim must be filed within 7 days of trip end) and automatic closure after 72 hours without customer response. Exception and claims handling standardizes types (provider no-show, customer late arrival, safety concern, pricing discrepancy), assigns responsibility (traveler/agent/provider/unknown), tracks investigation notes, evidence, compensation calculation, and enforces configurable SLAs (e.g., first response in 4 business hours, resolution in 3 days) with on-device reminders. All data is stored locally using Room (SQLite) with transactional writes for order updates, ticket status transitions, and quota adjustments to ensure consistency during crashes. The Kotlin app uses Android Views for high-performance screens and Koin for dependency injection; business logic is implemented in ViewModel/UseCase layers with all IO and computations off the main thread to avoid ANRs. Data quality validation includes field-level rules (date ranges cannot exceed 365 days, party size 1–12, enums for ticket type/status, USD amounts with two decimals), cross-record consistency checks (refund cannot exceed paid total), and deduplication using a fingerprint (content hash plus primary-key mapping) to suppress duplicates and quarantine anomalous entries with one-tap rollback to the last valid transaction. Content quality governance tracks local metrics for ratings, comments, favorites, and downloads; low-quality or duplicate content is automatically demoted and excluded from recommendations once thresholds are met (e.g., average rating <2.5 after 10 reviews or >80% hash similarity). Reporting, appeals, and review are handled as offline queues with audit logs. Security and privacy are enforced with data minimization, explicit on-device consent toggles, salted password hashing, encrypted storage for sensitive fields (e.g., traveler contact notes), masking in UI (show only last 4 digits of stored phone numbers), and retention rules (tickets retained 24 months; exports and deletions available per user). RecyclerView + DiffUtil must keep list scrolling at 60fps with 1,000+ records; large images are downsampled with an LRU cache to keep peak image memory under 20 MB; composite indexes target sub-50 ms queries on 100,000 local records. The app adapts to Android 10+ scoped storage and background restrictions, scheduling any maintenance (e.g., dedupe scans) via WorkManager only when the device is idle or charging.
